evals.name: slog-lazy-handler-init
evals.prompt: How does Go's log/slog handle lazy initialization of handlers? Or does it force creation through the handler's constructor functions? I want to know if there's a way to defer handler setup until the first log call.
evals.expected_output: Checks KB first (finds existing slog article but it doesn't cover lazy init), researches the topic, then saves findings to ~/.knowledge/ (general Go knowledge). Should create or update a KB article with sources.
expectations: Checked the knowledge base (.index files) before starting research
Found the existing slog article but recognized it doesn't cover lazy handler initialization
Performed actual research (web search, doc lookup, or code reading) to answer the question
Saved research findings to a .md file under ~/.knowledge/ (general KB, not projects/)
Created a .md.index sidecar alongside the article
Article frontmatter has sources but NOT title, last_modified, or tags
Index has title, last_modified, tags, summary, triggers, and sources fields
Response addresses whether slog supports lazy/deferred handler initialization

evals.name: otel-connector-component
evals.prompt: I'm looking at the OpenTelemetry Collector's connector component type -- the one that bridges between pipelines. How do connectors work, what are the stable vs experimental ones, and how do they differ from processors?
evals.expected_output: Checks KB first (nothing relevant), researches OTel connectors from current docs, saves findings to ~/.knowledge/ (general observability knowledge).
expectations: Checked the knowledge base (.index files) before starting research
Performed web research to find current information about OTel Collector connectors
Saved research findings to a .md file under ~/.knowledge/ (general KB, not projects/)
Created a .md.index sidecar alongside the article
Article frontmatter has sources with URLs (not just titles)
Index has title, last_modified, tags, summary, triggers, and sources fields
Response explains how connectors bridge between pipelines and how they differ from processors
Response includes information about connector stability levels (stable vs experimental)

evals.name: prometheus-upgrade-3.5-to-3.11
evals.prompt: We're upgrading Prometheus from 3.5 to 3.11. What are the breaking changes, deprecations, and notable new features across those releases? I need to know what to watch out for.
evals.expected_output: Checks KB first, researches Prometheus release notes from 3.5 through 3.11, saves a comprehensive upgrade guide to ~/.knowledge/ (general observability knowledge).
expectations: Checked the knowledge base (.index files) before starting research
Performed web research covering multiple Prometheus releases between 3.5 and 3.11
Saved research findings to a .md file under ~/.knowledge/ (general KB, not projects/)
Created a .md.index sidecar alongside the article
Article frontmatter has sources with URLs
Index has title, last_modified, tags, summary, triggers, and sources fields
Response covers breaking changes or deprecations across the upgrade path
Response covers notable new features introduced between 3.5 and 3.11

evals.name: prometheus-tsdb-head-compaction
evals.prompt: How does Prometheus's TSDB head block compaction work -- when does it trigger, what are the key code paths, and how does it interact with the WAL?
evals.expected_output: Checks KB first (nothing relevant), researches TSDB head compaction from upstream docs/source, saves findings to ~/.knowledge/ (general knowledge since this is upstream TSDB behavior, not fork-specific).
expectations: Checked the knowledge base (.index files) before starting research
Performed research (web search, upstream docs, or GitHub source reading) on TSDB head compaction
Saved research findings to a .md file under ~/.knowledge/ (general KB, not projects/)
Created a .md.index sidecar alongside the article
Article frontmatter has sources with URLs
Index has title, last_modified, tags, summary, triggers, and sources fields
Response explains when head block compaction triggers
Response explains how head compaction interacts with the WAL
